title: Why Dating Feels Confusing (It’s Not You) | Paul Eastwick
subtitle: how real attraction breaks the logic of preferences
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
most people date as though attraction is a matter of judgment.

you build a theory of what you want, give it a language: type, standards, preferences, non-negotiables… and assume that desire will behave accordingly. that if you know yourself well enough, and filter carefully enough, love will arrive in a form that makes sense on paper.

but the more interesting question is what happens when real attraction refuses to obey that theory.

that’s the tension running through this conversation with Paul Eastwick, whose work on attraction and romantic choice has spent years complicating some of the neatest stories people tell about love. his research doesn’t just challenge the idea that we know what we want but rather questions whether attraction is as stable, legible, and preference-driven as modern dating culture would like it to be. his new book, Bonded by Evolution, argues that some of our most confident assumptions about compatibility, mate choice, and romantic “types” don’t survive contact with real life.

Paul Eastwick’s work is interesting because it pushes against two convenient fantasies at once. the first is the fantasy of pure personal choice: that we calmly evaluate the field and select accordingly. the second is the fantasy of fixed evolutionary scripts: that men and women simply want predictable, opposing things. His work suggests something messier and, to me, more useful: that attraction is often contingent, situational, and shaped in ways we only recognize after the fact.

in this conversation, we talk about that gap between stated preference and actual desire, about why people’s checklists so often fail them, and about what modern dating gets wrong when it treats love like a market of sortable traits. we also get into a harder question underneath all of this: whether some of the confusion people feel in dating comes from assuming attraction should be more rational than it really is.

if you’ve ever been unsettled by the fact that the people you actually fall for rarely resemble the people you once claimed to want, this one will probably feel clarifying.

if this shifted how you think about attraction even slightly, you’ll probably find the rest of these conversations worth staying for. subscribe so you don’t miss the next one.

title: 20 ideas and habits for a multi-passionate life
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
a multi-passionate life sounds fancy until it is time to organize one. in theory, it is the dream:

a mind that ranges widely, a life with texture, curiosity that refuses to stay in one lane, the sort of person who can care deeply about books, design, business, dinner parties, psychology, music, scent, fitness, writing, and a completely niche interest in beautiful japanese stationery without feeling the need to apologize for any of it.

in practice, it can feel like living with eighteen browser tabs open in the soul where every interest is sincere, every idea seems to deserve a life and each new skill glints for a second like a possible future self. the difficulty is arranging all that appetite into something livable.

Subscribe

a good multipassionate life does not depend on turning every interest into a career or a side hustle or a five-year plan in a beige Notion dashboard but rather on knowing how to let your mind be large without letting your days become ridiculous. that takes a little structure, a little self-respect, and a willingness to stop treating every new fascination like a marriage proposal. here are twenty ideas and habits that make that kind of life feel less chaotic and far more satisfying:

1. keep one main thing. a multipassionate person still needs a center of gravity. one main project, primary craft, an area that gets the best energy first. everything else sits around that center more gracefully once the hierarchy is clear.

2. stop demanding equal attention from every interest. not every passion needs a season at the same time. some are daily, some are annual, some disappear for six months and return as though no time has passed.

3. build a life with categories. it may look like work passions, private passions, social passions, sensory passions, body passions. once your interests have somewhere to go, the whole inner world becomes less like a crowded party and more like a well-run house.

4. keep an active curiosity list. half the stress of being multipassionate comes from fearing you will forget what interests you. write it down. for example: skills to learn, topics to explore, books to read, places to visit, things to try. the mind relaxes when it knows its fascinations have been recorded.

5. learn to admire without immediately enrolling. every interesting thing is not your next project. some things are allowed to remain lovely from a distance. this habit alone can save a person from an astonishing amount of unnecessary overwhelm.

6. create small containers for your interests. a weekend class, a monthly solo ritual, one shelf, notebook, playlist, one evening a week. passions do better with containers than with grand declarations.

7. let hobbies stay hobbies. a life becomes spiritually cheap very quickly when every pleasure starts auditioning for monetization. some interests deserve to remain financially useless and emotionally excellent.

8. notice which passions are actually identities in disguise. sometimes the attraction is not the thing itself but the person it seems to promise you could become. that distinction matters. loving ceramics is different from loving the idea of being someone who does ceramics in a linen apron.

9. rotate inputs on purpose. a multipassionate mind gets stale when fed from one source only. books for a while, then films, then essays, then music, then conversations, then museums paired with a long walk with no phone. creative appetite likes variety more than discipline culture admits.

10. keep a few passions entirely offline. every interest does not need a witnesses. a private passion has a lovely effect on the personality. it restores intimacy between the self and the thing itself.

11. get good at being a beginner. multipassionate people start often, which means ego has to become less precious. awkwardness is part of the arrangement. one week you are competent, the next week you are googling how to hold the brush correctly.

12. choose depth in at least one area. breadth is beautiful but depth gives the life substance. one serious pursuit keeps the rest from floating off into purely decorative self-concept.

13. make peace with non-linearity. a multipassionate life mostly does not look tidy on paper. it loops, overlaps, pauses, resumes, cross-pollinates. the writer learns from hosting, the host learns from design, the designer learns from philosophy and so, the coherence often appears later.

14. keep a commonplacing habit. quotes, ideas, images, phrases, observations, strange facts, title ideas, questions worth returning to. multipassionate people think in connections, and a commonplace system gives those connections somewhere to live.

15. stop confusing novelty with meaning. newness is stimulating, but not every exciting thing deserves devotion. a little patience reveals which interests are genuine and which ones were just wearing good lighting.

16. arrange your space around who you actually are. visible instruments get practiced. accessible art supplies get used. books placed badly go unread. the environment can either support your range or subtly mock it.

17. develop tastes that talk to each other. the most satisfying multipassionate lives are not random collections of hobbies. they have a point of view. scent, interiors, food, fashion, writing, music, conversation can all begin to feel like parts of one larger sensibility.

18. protect unproductive time. curiosity does not thrive under constant usefulness. afternoons with no strategic purpose, aimless browsing in a bookstore, sketching badly, rearranging a shelf, reading something unrelated to your goals… all of this feeds the life in ways that are not instantly measurable.

19. answer the practical question early. which passions are for income? which are for identity? which are for pleasure? which are for community? once that is clear, a great deal of confusion disappears.

20. stop apologizing for range. some people build lovely lives through devotion to one thing. others come alive through a web of interests. neither arrangement is morally superior. the purpose of your passion is not to become more coherent for other people’s comfort but to build a life your mind can actually inhabit.

the multipassionate life works best when it stops trying to imitate the single-track one. different minds need different architectures. some people bloom through repetition, mastery, and narrowing. others stay vivid through layering, shifting, returning, collecting, making connections that look scattered from the outside and feel entirely natural from within. the real task is learning how to hold your range without turning it into noise. once that skill develops, a multi-passionate life stops feeling messy and starts feeling rich, which was the point of wanting one in the first place.

Comment

title: the psychology of eldest daughters
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
responsibility has a way of attaching itself to the eldest daughter so early that, by adulthood, it often stops feeling like a role and starts feeling like identity. from the outside, this reads as competence, maturity, leadership, the sort of girl relatives praise for being “so sensible.” from the inside, it can feel more complicated than that, because much of the skill comes from adaptation rather than ease. the internet calls this “eldest daughter syndrome,” but researchers and clinicians are clear that it is not a formal diagnosis, nor does birth order alone determine personality in any clean, destiny-like way. the term captures, at its best, captures a recognizable pattern: firstborn daughters are often expected to carry more, and many do so without much acknowledgment.

Subscribe

birth-order research is useful here mostly as a corrective to lazy storytelling. broad, lasting personality differences by birth order are smaller and less consistent than popular culture suggests, even though firstborns do show slight average advantages in educational attainment and measured or self-reported intellect in some studies. that matters, because the eldest-daughter story is about what families, cultures, and gender norms do with that first position. once “the oldest” gets translated into “the reliable one,” and “the reliable one” gets translated into “the girl who can handle it,” a great deal of family labor begins settling onto one nervous system.

research on sibling caregiving and parentification explains why so many eldest daughters recognize themselves in this pattern even when they would never use clinical language for it. one large study found adolescent girls were 42% more likely than boys to engage in sibling caretaking, which is an awfully efficient way to turn competence into expectation. parentification, the more formal term, refers to children taking on roles or responsibilities that properly belong to adults; this can mean practical tasks like caring for younger siblings, but it can also mean emotional jobs such as mediating conflict, comforting a parent, absorbing household tension, or becoming the unofficial regulator of everyone else’s feelings. that second category matters a great deal, because emotional parentification tends to be the harder one to spot and, in the literature, the more corrosive one over time.

a lot of eldest daughters grow up becoming fluent in a very particular emotional logic: love gets expressed through usefulness, safety comes from staying ahead of other people’s needs, and rest feels suspicious unless everything else has already been handled. this is where the psychology becomes interesting, because the same adaptations that make someone impressive at twenty-eight can make her tired in ways that are difficult to explain. hyper-competence looks elegant in public. privately, it can produce resentment, control issues, guilt around receiving help, and a low tolerance for watching other people do things badly and slowly, which is unfortunate, because that is how a great many people do things. one of the burdens of the eldest daughter is that she often becomes excellent at containing chaos while becoming less practiced at letting life be shared. competence becomes the costume, and then one day it is simply the skin.

none of this means the story is entirely bleak. parentification research is more nuanced than the internet usually is. some forms of responsibility can build maturity, self-efficacy, and relational skill when the load is temporary, age-appropriate, clearly named, and genuinely appreciated. the trouble begins when the labor becomes chronic, invisible, or emotionally inappropriate for the child carrying it. that distinction matters because many eldest daughters are often more capable, organized, and perceptive. the point is to ask what those strengths cost, and whether the family learned to admire them so much that no one noticed the overuse. studies on parentification repeatedly describe this “competence at a cost” pattern, where outward capability can sit right beside anxiety, shame, depression, overwork, or a persistent sense that one’s own needs are somehow badly timed.

adult life tends to expose the cracks in this arrangement. friendships become one-sided because caretaking feels easier than mutuality. romantic relationships get shaped by overfunctioning, then followed by fury when nobody notices how much invisible work is being done. family visits become regressions in sensible shoes. even success can feel oddly joyless when achievement has been fused with duty for too long. many eldest daughters are less afraid of hard work than of disappointing people, and even pressure than of stepping back and discovering that the whole family system was relying on their unpaid emotional administration. once that realization lands, the next task is loosening. asking, with some honesty, is this mine, or did i inherit it through repetition. letting someone else be uncomfortable without immediately translating their discomfort into personal responsibility. allowing help to simply happen before collapse makes it morally acceptable. learning that love does not become less real when it stops looking like management.

perhaps that is the most useful frame for understanding the psychology of eldest daughters: a pattern of adaptation built at the intersection of birth order, gender, family need, and social praise. the eldest daughter is often rewarded for being early to maturity, then gently punished for the same thing later when everyone assumes she requires less care than she actually does. once seen clearly, the goal is not to become less loving or less capable. a better goal is learning how to remain loving without becoming responsible for the emotional weather of every room. that shift is less flashy than healing narratives tend to promise, but much more useful. it gives the eldest daughter her personhood back from the role that taught her how to survive.

Comment

a few good places to read more

Northeastern University’s interview with Laurie Kramer on why “eldest daughter syndrome” resonates, even though it is not a diagnosis.

Rohrer et al. on birth order and personality, which is helpful if you want a less mythic, more evidence-based view of what birth order can and cannot explain.

Masiran et al.’s integrated review on parentification, and Borchet et al. on how different forms of parentification affect development and well-being.

title: This Is Why Motherhood Feels So Overwhelming | Dr. Nicole Pensak
subtitle: what’s really behind that constant sense of unease
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
some women become mothers and feel love immediately. some feel anxious, alert, overwhelmed, emotionally scrambled, or unlike themselves in ways they weren’t prepared for. a lot of that gets flattened into one vague idea “postpartum anxiety” but this conversation made a more useful distinction.

what Dr. Nikki Pensak talks about in rattled is that this period it’s a real transition where your brain and identity is changing and the way you move through the world changes with it. the hypervigilance, emotional intensity, the sense that something inside you has shifted… none of that is random, and none of it automatically means you’re failing at motherhood. her work frames the postpartum brain as being in reconstruction, not simply in crisis.

in this conversation, we talk about why new mothers can feel so different after having a baby, why that feeling is often misunderstood, and why so many women silently assume something is wrong with them when what they’re experiencing is a profound transition that deserves language, context, and support. dr. pensak also argues that mothers often become the “silent patient,” with attention directed toward the baby while the mother’s own distress goes unseen.

if you’ve ever had the feeling that motherhood changed you more than people admitted it would, this one will probably resonate.

title: how to increase willpower
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
willpower has been sold to people in a very insulting way, as though the whole thing were simply a matter of character, a private moral resource possessed in abundance by those who wake up early, answer emails promptly, and feel no emotional attachment to snacks, avoidance, or the false promise of “starting fresh on monday.” this has always seemed like a strange explanation for such a common human difficulty. if willpower were merely a virtue issue, half the population would not be standing in the kitchen at 4:40 p.m. bargaining with themselves about whether to do the task, skip the task, postpone the task, reinvent their entire life, or just eat something with unnecessary conviction.

Subscribe

willpower is hard to understand because it hardly disappears in the areas that flatter the ego. people can summon astonishing discipline for things that feel urgent, visible, emotionally charged, or tied to identity. replying to the message that matters, showing up for a child, hitting a deadline that has consequences, helping someone in need, preserving an image of competence in public: suddenly the body finds reserves. the difficulty shows up more often in the private, repetitive, slightly dull corners of life, where nobody is clapping and the task does not come with adrenaline, novelty, or a satisfying sense of self-importance. this is why the conversation around willpower becomes so unhelpful so quickly. the problem is that willpower is fragile when it is forced to do the work that structure, energy, clarity, and emotional steadiness should have been doing all along.

a great deal of what people call low willpower is really decision fatigue wearing bad lighting. by the time a person gets to the task they keep failing to do, the mind has already been making choices for hours, small ones, annoying ones, invisible ones, all of them taking a bite out of the internal bandwidth needed to act cleanly. what to wear, what to answer, what to buy, what to make, what to postpone, what to tolerate, what to say, what to ignore, whether that tone in the message meant anything, whether dinner requires effort, whether the day is recoverable. eventually the brain stops feeling like a wise executive and starts resembling underpaid staff. then comes the self-criticism, which is unhelpful because shame has never been a reliable source of stamina. people often imagine willpower as some heroic force of refusal, when in ordinary life it looks far more like reducing the number of unnecessary decisions before the important one arrives.

energy matters more than most ambition admits. lack of sleep makes everything feel negotiable in the worst way. hunger makes discipline sound strangely oppressive. emotional strain can flatten the desire to act long before the task itself begins. an overstimulated nervous system has very little interest in noble effort; it wants relief, speed, comfort, escape, or any combination of the four. under those conditions, asking for more willpower is like asking someone to write a decent paragraph in the middle of airport turbulence. this is why people keep misreading themselves. they assume they need stronger standards, when the far more honest answer is that the body is depleted and the mind has started preferring whatever requires the least friction. nothing improves from that discovery except accuracy, but accuracy is often where useful change begins.

the people with the most consistent follow-through are rarely engaged in minute-by-minute acts of self-conquest. their advantage is usually less fancy. they remove negotiations, make certain choices earlier and narrow the moments in which mood gets a vote. they know that a task debated too many times begins acquiring emotional grandeur it never deserved. a workout starts sounding existential. a writing session begins to feel like a referendum on talent. answering one email picks up the mood of wartime diplomacy. all of this makes action heavier than it needs to be. willpower increases, oddly enough, when life contains fewer opportunities to hold a committee meeting with the self. once the decision is settled in advance, the day becomes less psychological.

identity has a role here too, though not in the inflated way self-help language tends to present it. a person who believes she is constantly battling herself will experience every task as proof of an unstable inner government. a person who begins thinking of herself as someone who follows through a decent amount of the time will often behave differently because the action stops feeling so unfamiliar. repetition changes the story. one completed promise to the self does not create transformation, but it does create evidence, and evidence is much more persuasive than inspiration. small completions matter for this reason. they tighten the link between intention and behavior. a mind that has seen itself act becomes easier to trust.

difficulty enters when people make willpower responsible for too many impossible jobs. it cannot compensate forever for a life with no margins, rhythm, rest, systems, emotional honesty or realistic pacing. it does not thrive under chaos, and it becomes especially unreliable when every habit is built around ideal conditions. discipline that only works when motivation is high and the weather is pleasing and nobody has sent an annoying text by noon is not really discipline at all. sturdier forms of willpower grow in less flattering soil: ordinary routines, earlier bedtimes, fewer decisions, more protein, clearer boundaries, a simpler environment, less fantasy about becoming a different species of person. the boring answer has disappointed many people, including me, yet it remains the one most likely to hold.

the deepest shift, though, comes from relating to willpower with less melodrama. failing to do the thing one evening does not reveal a ruined character. struggling with consistency does not make a person unserious. every human life contains friction between what is wanted in the long term and what feels easier in the moment. that tension is not a personal defect. it is the texture of being alive inside a body that likes comfort and a mind that tires easily. the aim is not to become iron-willed in some punishing, joyless sense. a better goal is becoming easier to direct, less available for endless internal debate, less dependent on moods that change hourly, and more willing to set up a life that does not ask heroism from the self every afternoon.

willpower, in the end, becomes stronger when it is treated less like a personality trait and more like a resource that can be protected, supported, and used with some intelligence. that tends to mean fewer grand vows, fewer emotional negotiations, and a little more respect for the ordinary conditions under which people actually succeed.

Comment

title: 75 solo artist dates to try
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
of all the ideas in the artist’s way, the artist date is the one that seems the most charming when you first hear it and the most revealing once you actually try to do it. julia cameron describes it as a weekly solo expedition meant to fill the well, which is a lovely phrase until you realize how bad many adults have become at unstructured delight. i’m in week one of the book, and this list became my version of research, partly for myself and partly for anyone reading who wants inspiration they can borrow, adapt, steal, improve, or use to invent their own. the real point of an artist date is not to become more cultured in a way that sounds good in conversation, nor to post a well-lit carousel that suggests one is in a committed relationship with beauty. the point is contact. contact with attention, novelty, taste, play, with the odd little corners of the self that do not come out during admin, errands, inboxes, laundry, deadlines, or the kind of adult efficiency that slowly flattens a personality.

Subscribe

an artist date, in my opinion, should feel slightly unnecessary in the best way. a little impractical. a little specific. something that gives the mind new material without demanding a deliverable at the end. no pressure to turn it into content, to summarize the insight, no requirement to come home transformed into a woman who finally understands color theory and her childhood. a good artist date simply makes life feel more textured. that is already enough. so here are 75 ideas, ranging from genuinely useful to mildly niche, which is often where the best ones live:

1. go to a bookstore with one rule: only browse sections you normally ignore.

2. sit in a hotel lobby and write down everything people are wearing.

3. visit a flower shop and ask which stems look the most overlooked that day.

4. buy one magazine purely for the advertisements.

5. wander through a kitchenware store and choose the object with the most personality.

6. take yourself to a bakery and judge pastries like an unreasonably sincere food critic.

7. go to a thrift store and invent lives for five things you almost buy.

8. spend an hour in a fabric shop touching textures and naming the moods they belong to.

9. visit a museum gift shop without entering the museum first.

10. go to a garden center and choose a plant based entirely on emotional chemistry.

11. take a different route through your own neighborhood and act like a visitor with excellent observational skills.

12. go to the nicest stationery store you can find and buy one absurdly specific thing.

13. sit in a café and make a list called “titles for things i have not written yet.”

14. walk through a grocery store and notice what packaging feels weirdly beautiful.

15. visit a perfume counter and write a one-line character sketch for every scent you try.

16. go to the library and pick up the kind of art book that usually intimidates you.

17. spend an afternoon looking at old postcards online or in a flea market.

18. take yourself to a matinee screening of something you would never normally choose.

19. go window shopping in a part of town that is financially none of your business.

20. visit a pet shop or aquarium store and stare at creatures with highly specific faces.

21. make a solo trip to a home store and notice what kind of room you are secretly trying to become.

22. order a drink purely because the name sounds literary or faintly ridiculous.

23. go to a hardware store and admire the accidental poetry of practical objects.

24. spend an hour in a record store asking yourself which album covers still have authority.

25. visit a farmer’s market and buy the ingredient you know least how to use.

26. walk through a children’s bookstore and remember what used to feel like magic before taste became so managed.

27. go to a pottery studio or ceramic shop and study bowls as though they are moral philosophy.

28. visit a vintage shop and try on one thing that belongs to a different version of your life.

29. sit on a bench somewhere beautiful and eavesdrop with tenderness rather than malice.

30. go to a specialty tea shop and choose a blend based on the name alone.

31. take yourself out for breakfast on a weekday and bring a notebook instead of a phone.

32. walk into an art supply store and buy the color you are currently missing.

33. visit a local exhibition and spend longer with the piece you dislike than the one you immediately understand.

34. go to a fruit market and photograph the color combinations that feel editorial.

35. take a bus somewhere for no strong reason and observe the city changing outside the window.

36. browse recipe books as if they are really books about mood and ambition.

37. spend an hour collecting overheard phrases that sound like essay titles.

38. go to an antique store and choose the item that feels most haunted by glamour.

39. visit a music shop and ask what beginners buy when they think they are becoming a new person.

40. go to a cake shop and pick the dessert that has the most emotional intelligence.

41. choose one street and photograph every doorway that seems to belong in a novel.

42. visit a secondhand bookstore and buy the messiest copy of something beloved.

43. go to a toy store and notice which part of your imagination still responds immediately.

44. spend a morning at the beach, park, or waterfront making a list of visual details instead of thoughts.

45. go to a flea market with a spending limit low enough to keep things interesting.

46. visit an independent cinema and read all the posters like a woman selecting her next emotional era.

47. sit in the lobby of a fancy building and study floral arrangements as though they are trying to tell you something.

48. browse cookbooks from one country and notice which meals feel like atmosphere rather than food.

49. visit a bookstore café and read the first page of seven books you do not intend to buy.

50. go to a music performance, poetry reading, or tiny local event where nobody is trying too hard to be discovered.

51. take yourself to a used furniture store and decide which object has survived the most.

52. spend an afternoon making a tiny map of places in your city that still give you a sense of possibility.

53. visit a bakery in a neighborhood you do not usually go to and judge the wallpaper as seriously as the croissants.

54. go to a florist and ask which flower people stop buying once they become adults.

55. wander through a department store beauty section and note which product names sound like failed novels.

56. go to a photo booth and take pictures purely for your future self’s amusement.

57. spend an hour in a public place sketching layouts, not objects, just the arrangement of space.

58. visit a local market and buy the ingredient with the prettiest name.

59. go to a bookstore and build an imaginary syllabus for your current life crisis.

60. take yourself for a solo lunch and bring a pen good enough to make handwriting feel aspirational.

61. visit a church, temple, mosque, or old building with a strong sense of age and sit there without trying to extract a lesson.

62. go to a stationery aisle and choose one notebook for a very niche category of thought.

63. spend an hour looking at movie stills from a decade you love and write down recurring visual motifs.

64. visit a spice shop and let scent do the work that language usually tries to overmanage.

65. go to a small gallery opening where free wine and uncertainty are both present.

66. take a long walk after rain and look at the city as a surface first and a system second.

67. browse children’s art supplies and buy the version of creativity that seems least respectable.

68. go to a lighting store and admit how much mood depends on lamps.

69. spend an afternoon in a library reading old issues of magazines from another era.

70. visit a store that sells beautiful nonsense, which is to say, objects nobody technically needs but many people spiritually require.

71. go to a museum and choose one painting to write a fake backstory for.

72. buy a single ingredient, flower, or object solely because it looks like the beginning of a thought.

73. visit an open house or furniture showroom and notice what kind of life the room is trying to sell.

74. spend an hour making a list called “things i like that do not improve me.”

75. take yourself somewhere small and lovely, stay longer than necessary, and resist the urge to turn the whole thing into productivity.

what i like about artist dates, at least from this early point in the artist’s way, is that they restore seriousness to seemingly unserious things: taste, attention and delight matter. following a whim into a bookstore, a flower market, hotel lobby, or a bakery may not look like the kind of habit that changes a life, but so much of creativity is built from material gathered before anyone realizes it is material. a line overheard in passing, color combinations, different rooms, a scent, a ridiculous object in a secondhand shop, the shape of a cake, the title of a book you never buy. the inner life is fed in strange ways. adults tend to forget this because usefulness has become our favorite religion, and artist dates interrupt that mood just enough to let a person remember she is not only here to function.

Comment

so that is my week one research offering: 75 places to begin, with full permission to alter them, combine them, cheapen them, elevate them, or invent your own entirely. the point is to leave the house, or even just the usual mental route, and go meet something that makes the mind feel a little less housebroken.

title: 7 things that make someone an excellent conversationalist
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
good conversation has been misbranded as charm for far too long, which is convenient for charming people and deeply unhelpful for everyone else who keeps wondering why certain interactions feel nourishing while others feel like polite endurance.

Subscribe

the difference hardly comes down to how interesting someone is, or how many stories they can carry into a room, whether they know the correct cultural references at the correct time. something else is happening in the background, more structural and far less performative. certain people understand, almost intuitively, that conversation is not an opportunity to display a finished self, but a place where thinking is allowed to happen in real time, with another person present as witness rather than judge. time with them tends to feel slightly expansive, as though language itself had loosened its grip, and what remains at the end of the evening is not the memory of what was said but the feeling of having been received without unnecessary friction:

1. a willingness to let another person arrive

most people interrupt in ways that are socially acceptable and psychologically intrusive, which is a polite way of saying they do not always notice how quickly they step into another person’s sentence before it has found its shape. an excellent conversationalist resists this urge without making a ceremony of it, allowing pauses to exist without rushing to rescue them, giving unfinished thoughts enough space to become something clearer. this creates an atmosphere in which speaking feels less like performing competence and more like thinking aloud, and in that shift, people often become more articulate than they believed themselves to be. silence, handled properly, becomes part of the conversation rather than a gap to be filled.

2. questions that move the conversation sideways

ordinary questions tend to push for information, which is efficient but hardly memorable, while better questions alter the angle of the exchange in a way that opens new territory without demanding it. a slight change in phrasing can turn a predictable answer into something more reflective, textured and human. asking what surprised someone rather than what happened, what they keep thinking about rather than what they are doing next, what felt different rather than what was good or bad. these are small shifts that change the quality of attention in the room. curiosity, when it is specific enough, becomes an invitation rather than an interrogation.

3. an instinct for staying with something longer

conversation often suffers from a kind of restlessness that masquerades as momentum, where ideas are introduced and then abandoned before they have had time to develop any real depth. an excellent conversationalist lingers in a way that feels natural rather than forced, following a thread just a little further than expected, asking one more question when most people would have moved on, noticing when something interesting has been said and allowing it to expand. this creates continuity, which in turn builds trust, because it signals that what is being said matters enough to be explored rather than skimmed.

4. contributions that build on what exists

there is a familiar pattern in conversation where one person shares something and another responds with a story of their own, slightly larger in scale, slightly more intense, unintentionally shifting the center of the exchange. excellent conversationalists avoid this drift without withholding themselves. they contribute in a way that feels connected to what has already been said, adding texture instead of replacing the subject entirely. the effect is cumulative. ideas begin to layer rather than compete. the conversation becomes something shared rather than a sequence of individual performances.

5. a relaxed relationship with being interesting

pressure to be engaging tends to produce the opposite result, creating interactions that feel curated rather than alive, where every sentence carries a faint awareness of how it might be received. excellent conversationalists are not preoccupied with managing their own image in this way. interest is directed outward, toward the exchange itself, which paradoxically makes them more engaging. when a person is not trying to secure approval through every remark, their presence becomes easier to be around. the conversation breathes more naturally. humor appears without effort. small details are allowed to exist without being polished into anecdotes.

6. an ability to register emotional tone

every conversation carries more than its literal content. there is mood, hesitation, emphasis, the slight tightening or loosening of language when something matters. an excellent conversationalist notices these shifts without turning them into a project. when someone speaks carefully, they respond with care rather than urgency. when something light is being shared, they do not weigh it down with unnecessary seriousness. this kind of attunement is not about analyzing the other person but about responding proportionately, which is a much subtler skill. it prevents the conversation from becoming either too flat or too intense, both of which can make people withdraw.

7. a tolerance for imperfection in the exchange itself

excellent conversationalists allow for this without trying to fix it, understanding that awkwardness is not a failure but a natural part of interaction. this tolerance removes a surprising amount of pressure, making it easier for people to remain present instead of monitoring their own performance. when conversation is allowed to be slightly uneven, it often becomes more genuine, and in that genuineness, more engaging.

a disposition emerges from these qualities which is a way of approaching other people that prioritizes presence over performance. conversation stops feeling like something to get right and starts feeling like something to participate in. the difference is subtle but transformative. people remember how they felt in a conversation long after they forget the specifics of what was said, and the feeling of having been met without being managed, listened to without being redirected, and allowed to think without being rushed is one that tends to linger.

and that, in the end, is what makes someone excellent at it which is the ability to make another person feel slightly more like themselves in their own company.

Comment

ordinary questions tend to push for information, which is efficient but hardly memorable, while better questions alter the angle of the exchange in a way that opens new territory without demanding it. a slight change in phrasing can turn a predictable answer into something more reflective, textured and human. asking what surprised someone rather than what happened, what they keep thinking about rather than what they are doing next, what felt different rather than what was good or bad. these are small shifts that change the quality of attention in the room. curiosity, when it is specific enough, becomes an invitation rather than an interrogation.
3. an instinct for staying with something longer

conversation often suffers from a kind of restlessness that masquerades as momentum, where ideas are introduced and then abandoned before they have had time to develop any real depth. an excellent conversationalist lingers in a way that feels natural rather than forced, following a thread just a little further than expected, asking one more question when most people would have moved on, noticing when something interesting has been said and allowing it to expand. this creates continuity, which in turn builds trust, because it signals that what is being said matters enough to be explored rather than skimmed.
4. contributions that build on what exists

there is a familiar pattern in conversation where one person shares something and another responds with a story of their own, slightly larger in scale, slightly more intense, unintentionally shifting the center of the exchange. excellent conversationalists avoid this drift without withholding themselves. they contribute in a way that feels connected to what has already been said, adding texture instead of replacing the subject entirely. the effect is cumulative. ideas begin to layer rather than compete. the conversation becomes something shared rather than a sequence of individual performances.
5. a relaxed relationship with being interesting

pressure to be engaging tends to produce the opposite result, creating interactions that feel curated rather than alive, where every sentence carries a faint awareness of how it might be received. excellent conversationalists are not preoccupied with managing their own image in this way. interest is directed outward, toward the exchange itself, which paradoxically makes them more engaging. when a person is not trying to secure approval through every remark, their presence becomes easier to be around. the conversation breathes more naturally. humor appears without effort. small details are allowed to exist without being polished into anecdotes.
6. an ability to register emotional tone

every conversation carries more than its literal content. there is mood, hesitation, emphasis, the slight tightening or loosening of language when something matters. an excellent conversationalist notices these shifts without turning them into a project. when someone speaks carefully, they respond with care rather than urgency. when something light is being shared, they do not weigh it down with unnecessary seriousness. this kind of attunement is not about analyzing the other person but about responding proportionately, which is a much subtler skill. it prevents the conversation from becoming either too flat or too intense, both of which can make people withdraw.
7. a tolerance for imperfection in the exchange itself

excellent conversationalists allow for this without trying to fix it, understanding that awkwardness is not a failure but a natural part of interaction. this tolerance removes a surprising amount of pressure, making it easier for people to remain present instead of monitoring their own performance. when conversation is allowed to be slightly uneven, it often becomes more genuine, and in that genuineness, more engaging.

a disposition emerges from these qualities which is a way of approaching other people that prioritizes presence over performance. conversation stops feeling like something to get right and starts feeling like something to participate in. the difference is subtle but transformative. people remember how they felt in a conversation long after they forget the specifics of what was said, and the feeling of having been met without being managed, listened to without being redirected, and allowed to think without being rushed is one that tends to linger.
and that, in the end, is what makes someone excellent at it which is the ability to make another person feel slightly more like themselves in their own company.

title: 10 weird habits for creativity
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
creativity has a reputation problem. people keep dressing it up as inspiration, talent, genius, artistic temperament, the right notebook, the right desk, the right hour of the morning, the right playlist, the right amount of discipline, and occasionally the right candle. meanwhile, a great deal of creative work is produced by people doing things that would look unremarkable, mildly irrational, or faintly embarrassing from the outside as they pace. they eavesdrop, rename files badly, collect odd little phrases like magpies with a literature degree. they tend to delay the real task by orbiting it until the brain gives up and opens the door. in that spirit, here are ten habits that are less polished than the average productivity system and far more alive. these sound slightly made up when you explain them to another person, which is usually how you know they may actually be useful:

Subscribe

1. keeping an “almost useless” notes app

it shouldn’t be anything that makes your creativity look like a startup. i mean a messy, undignified stash of fragments you are not yet clever enough to use which could look like: a sentence somebody said in a lift, a title with no essay attached, a thought that arrived while brushing your teeth and currently makes no sense in daylight. creativity hardly happens as a complete argument. people often miss this because they are waiting for fully formed brilliance instead of collecting partial heat. the almost useless notes app matters because it teaches you to respect the unfinished thing. half of being creative is noticing when your mind has brushed against something alive before it disappears.

2. talking to yourself like you are being interviewed for a magazine profile nobody asked for

this is humiliating, which is part of its charm. walk around your kitchen and explain your idea out loud as though a journalist has just asked, “so what are you actually trying to say here?” something about spoken language exposes weak thinking very quickly. the parts that sounded intelligent in your head suddenly collapse under the weight of air. but the opposite also happens. you stumble into phrasing that is warmer, funnier, precise, human than anything you would have produced while staring nobly at a blinking cursor. some ideas need the body involved. they need pacing, tone, eye rolls, hand gestures, mild delusion. the page is useful. the mouth is sometimes smarter.

3. changing locations before changing ideas

one of the least glamorous truths about creativity is that sometimes you do not have a thinking problem. you probably have a wallpaper problem. after a while, the same room begins to give your thoughts the same shape every time. this is why people suddenly become convinced of their own originality in hotel lobbies and corner cafés. a different setting interrupts the stale relationship between your mind and your habits. even moving from a desk to the floor can do it. the point is not aesthetic wandering for its own sake but to stop expecting one environment to generate every version of you. your brain likes novelty more than your routine admits.

4. letting yourself become mildly obsessed with one tiny thing

creativity often improves the moment you stop trying to be broadly inspired and start becoming oddly fixated on just one small object of fascination such as: vintage perfume names, motel fonts, wedding forums, old menus, the way people decorate refrigerators in movies from the early 2000s. the texture of obsession matters because it gives your mind somewhere sticky to go. people become dull when they only consume information they can justify. the weird little side fascination is often where your style starts forming.

5. doing the task badly on purpose first

perfectionism loves to disguise itself as standards, taste, integrity, discernment, or being “very particular.” sometimes it is simply fear wearing better language. one of the best creative habits is to begin in a way that would mildly offend your future self. so, go ahead and write the ugly version, draft the obvious title, sketch the structure that feels too plain, make something with enough shape to argue with. this works because the creative block is often an excess of self-surveillance. a bad first attempt gives the mind something to push against, and pushing against something is far easier than creating from an elegant void.

6. collecting people

a lot of people build mood boards and forget to build a world. references help, of course, but creativity also comes from being haunted by people. the friend who describes a breakup like a legal dispute. the aunt with suspiciously strong opinions about napkins. the man in line at the pharmacy buying three obscure items and looking spiritually defeated. a person who says “goodness me” without irony has contributed more to some creative projects than an hour of trend research. once you start paying attention to human specificity, your work gets better because your material gets warmer. ideas alone can become sterile. people give them blood pressure.

7. giving your brain a decoy task

this is useful for anyone whose best thoughts only appear while doing something unrelated and slightly inconvenient such as folding laundry, walking to buy toothpaste, reorganizing a drawer you did not care about yesterday, taking a shower that becomes suspiciously long once the good sentence arrives. the mind often loosens when it is not being directly watched. ask it for brilliance at your desk and it becomes bureaucratic. send it off to rinse a coffee cup and suddenly it remembers how to think. a decoy task lowers the pressure just enough for the subconscious to stop acting like a sulky employee. some of your best ideas do not want to occur at the meeting. they want to sneak in through the service entrance.

8. reading things a little adjacent to your actual work

the obvious move is to consume more from your own category. if you write essays, read more essays. if you make videos, study more videos. and yes, do that. but not exclusively, or your work starts sounding like it was raised by cousins. adjacent reading is often where freshness enters: philosophy can improve product writing, gossip can improve cultural criticism, memoir can improve business content, fashion criticism can improve essay structure. the interesting mind is usually the one with slightly illegal cross-pollination habits. creativity thrives when one part of your brain says, “this should not be relevant,” and another part says, “watch me.”

9. leaving something unfinished on purpose

finishing everything cleanly sounds virtuous, but it can make the next day harder than necessary. one weirdly effective habit is to stop while you still know what comes next: leave a sentence half-built, leave the outline open, leave yourself a visible trail back into the work. this matters because starting is often the most psychologically expensive part of any creative act. an unfinished thread reduces the drama of re-entry. instead of returning to a blank page and a vague identity crisis, you return to momentum. the mind likes an open loop. it continues tugging at it in the background, which means your work keeps living a little even when you have gone off to behave like a person with other responsibilities.

10. paying attention to envy without making it your personality

envy is not elegant, but it is informative. the work that makes you slightly irritable is often showing you something you want. not necessarily to copy, and certainly not to stalk under the banner of “research,” but to decode. maybe you do not want their exact style; maybe you want their freedom, their clarity, their scale, their nerve, their willingness to be silly in public, their refusal to edit away the interesting part. the mature creative move is to treat envy less like a moral failing and more like contraband data. it points toward the places where your own taste, ambition, or unlived ideas are trying to get your attention.

none of these habits are especially glamorous, which is annoying because people love to talk about creativity as though it enters in a silk robe with a beautiful morning routine. more often it looks like fragments, obsession, awkward voice notes, accidental insight, messy notes, bad drafts, odd fixations, and an increasing tolerance for your own imperfect process. creativity is not always a sign of brilliance. sometimes it is simply what happens when you let your mind behave a little strangely without punishing it back into efficiency.

and perhaps that is the useful thing to remember. the goal is not to become a more optimized creative person with a better system and a nicer lamp, though i support the lamp. the goal is to build a life where your mind has enough room to notice, wander, collect, misbehave, and return with something worth keeping. that usually requires less polishing than people think and a little more permission to be weird in peace.

title: why individualism makes it harder to be a villager
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
individualism has excellent branding. it seems like it’s dressed as freedom, self-respect, healthy boundaries, personal choice, moving to the city you like, dating people who “align,” buying the right lamp, learning your attachment style, and finally becoming the kind of adult who knows what works for them. it sounds intelligent, modern, like something a person should want after centuries of being told to shrink, obey, marry sensibly, and call it fulfillment. of course we wanted out of that arrangement and the promise of becoming a fully formed person, answerable mainly to themselves, felt like progress. and in many ways, it was. i am not here to make some suspicious little speech about how people should return to being communal in a way that mostly means useful to everybody else. i mean that many of us have built lives around independence and still find ourselves deeply tired by how much life now requires us to carry alone.

Subscribe

the modern self is forever in management: managing time, mood, spending, communication, expectations, access to the self, which now apparently has to be protected like a members-only rooftop. a person can spend half their week just maintaining the conditions under which they are able to function and because this all gets explained in the language of growth, it can take a while to notice that the whole arrangement is exhausting. but one day you realize your life has become a private corporation and you are employed by it in several unpaid roles: founder, assistant, therapist, logistics coordinator, emotional support animal. no wonder everybody is a little brittle.

this is why the idea of a village keeps haunting people who would never describe themselves as needy. the village is not really about rustic charm or some pinterest-board fantasy of bread, baskets, and women named eliza helping one another shell peas. it is about shared weight. it is not being the only witness to your life. the village does not care whether your boundaries are beautifully articulated but the village asks whether anybody can come over when your day falls apart at 4:17 p.m.

the problem is that individualism makes us fluent in preference and a little clumsy at participation. we know what we like, what drains us, which people feel expansive and which ones feel vaguely bad for our nervous system. we have become connoisseurs of fit and while some of that discernment is hard-won and necessary, it has also made many people less tolerant of the ordinary friction that comes with real communal life. a village is full of people who are occasionally annoying. they are late, too chatty, emotionally repetitive, bad at texting, too fond of advice, weird about money, unreliable in one category and heroic in another. one woman will overstep every boundary in conversation and then appear at your door with medicine before you have even admitted you are sick. another will never remember your birthday and will sit with you in the hospital for six hours. villages are inconvenient in this deeply human way. they are made of mixed records and inconsistent personalities. social media has convinced people they should only keep emotionally efficient relationships, but that is not how a village works. a village works because people keep showing up with all their unevenness intact.

and this is where modern life gets almost funny. everyone says they want community, but community is not a scented candle you order after deciding your apartment needs warmth. community is repetitive. it requires you to answer the phone when you are tired, go to the birthday you do not fully feel like attending, help someone move, remember the name of a neighbour’s mother, make an extra portion, lend the charger, ask twice, forgive minor offences, and survive being perceived in less than ideal conditions. the modern personality wants support with minimal entanglement. a lovely idea. impossible, unfortunately. a person cannot be deeply held by people they only interact with at their own convenience.

part of the difficulty is that individualism has trained us to turn every need into a personal project. lonely? work on your relationship with yourself. overwhelmed? refine your systems. unsupported? communicate better. unhappy? get clearer on your values. again, none of this is useless. some of it is wise. but after a certain point it starts to resemble one long cultural attempt to reframe collective problems as private assignments. this would all be very inspiring if it were not so obviously false. some burdens are too heavy for one person, even a self-aware one with electrolytes and nice handwriting.

if this is useful, the paid tier is where it gets practical.
you’ll get the full frameworks and step-by-step breakdowns, plus access to the private milk and cookies podcast: long-form deep dives, video essays, early access to every episode, and guest interviews just for subscribers.
join and go deeper.

i think this is why so many adults feel both competent and faintly abandoned. they know how to book the appointment, order the groceries, read the article, research the supplement, rearrange the calendar, and say the emotionally literate thing in the group chat. they are perfectly capable people. they are also secretly hoping somebody might notice they are tired without requiring a calendar link and a twelve-point explanation. underneath all the language of self-sufficiency sits a very ordinary longing. to be part of a web of reciprocal noticing and to live in such a way that help is not an emergency measure but part of the atmosphere.

that is why individualism makes it harder to be a villager. it tells us to build a life that reflects us, protects us, expresses us, and answers to us. admirable goals, all of them. but village life asks for slightly less polishing and a little more tolerance. it asks you to accept that people will matter to you in messy, imperfect, deeply inconvenient ways, to be interruptible; and to stop treating dependence like a character defect dressed in bad lighting.

perhaps the more tender truth is that most people never wanted radical independence as much as they wanted dignity within connection. they wanted room to become themselves without being swallowed paired with choice without exile while wanting freedom and familiarity in the same life, which is not childish or contradictory or evidence of emotional confusion. it is a very old human desire. the village was about creating enough shared structure that the self did not have to carry every single thing alone.

and that maybe the correction. only a softer understanding of what a good life requires. more mutuality, ordinary usefulness, chances to be known beyond your curated best with less pride in carrying everything unassisted. because the truth is, wanting a village is not a sign that you are failing at adulthood. it may be the first sane response to living in a culture that keeps asking one person to do the work of many.

title: contrarian friends are exhausting
author: ayushithakkar
raw_article:
some friendships take time before they start to feel tiring as a certain pattern settles in so gradually that it’s almost mistaken for personality at first, the kind of dynamic where conversations never quite land where they begin and something you say is gently turned, adjusted, reframed into a slightly different shape. while none of those individual moments feel significant enough to question, the accumulation of them creates this underlying sense that nothing gets to stay simple for long, that even the most casual thoughts are pulled into a kind of reworking that makes the interaction feel more effortful than it should be.

Subscribe

it’s easy to admire this kind of presence in the beginning because it reads as someone who is paying attention with a friend who isn’t satisfied with surface-level agreement, maybe they also notice the angles other people miss, and that can feel refreshing in a way that’s hard to find, especially when most conversations move too quickly and settle too easily, so there’s a period where it feels like you’re in something sharper, more engaged and a conversation that has weight to it. but that weight can be mistaken for depth, because it asks more of you, it holds your thoughts in place longer than usual, making you consider things from different directions, and all of that can feel valuable enough that you don’t immediately notice what it’s taking in return.

this almost frustrating feeling is felt across many small ones, in the way a light comment becomes something that needs clarification, the way a simple preference turns into something that needs to be explained and even agreement feels like it occurs after a process rather than naturally. on the surface none of this feels like conflict because nothing truly escalates, or breaks, but the conversation starts to feel less like something you’re participating in and more like something you’re navigating, where each thought has to be placed carefully if you want it to hold its position for more than a few seconds. over time, that navigation becomes internal.

you start anticipating the shift before it happens, in a way your sentences begin to form differently, the way you soften certain things before saying them, the way you leave other things out entirely because holding onto them would take more energy than they’re worth, and this adjustment feels small at first, almost practical, but it changes how you show up in a way that becomes harder to ignore, because you’re no longer speaking from the same place, you’re speaking from a version of yourself that is already accounting for what will happen next.

that anticipation is where the exhaustion begins to take shape, because it introduces a constant layer of management into something that should feel more natural, and even though each individual exchange remains reasonable, even thoughtful on the surface, the overall experience becomes heavier than expected, as if the conversation is asking for a level of engagement that doesn’t quite give anything back, and that imbalance is difficult to name because it doesn’t come from anything overtly wrong but from something that is technically working.

this is what makes contrarian friendships so hard to talk about, because the behavior itself is defensible, even admirable in certain contexts, and pushing against it can easily make you seem like the one who is avoiding depth or resisting challenge, which most people don’t want to be seen as, so the adjustment continues, the conversation continues, and the pattern deepens without ever being directly addressed.

the ease that should ideally be found in comforting friendship is lost in such situations; the kind of ease that allows a thought to exist without needing to be strengthened or corrected, that lets a conversation move somewhere unexpected without immediately pulling it back into a more controlled shape, and once that ease disappears, everything begins to feel slightly contained, slightly overworked, as if the interaction is being held too tightly to allow anything new to emerge from it. and that containment changes how the entire relationship feels in the way you begin to approach it, the way you prepare for conversations without meaning to, how you feel a small sense of relief when certain topics don’t come up, the way you notice how much easier it is to stay within a narrower range of expression, and that narrowing becomes the most telling part, because it shows how much the dynamic has shifted without needing to point to a specific moment where it happened.

eventually, the exhaustion stops being about what’s being said at all, and starts being about what never gets the chance to be said, the thoughts that are filtered before they’re spoken under the directions the conversation could have taken but didn’t, in the sense that everything is being shaped into something more precise but less alive, and that absence becomes more noticeable than anything that is actually present.

that’s usually when the realization lands, gradually, that it isn’t disagreement that’s tiring, it’s the lack of rest inside it, the sense that nothing is allowed to settle with every moment being held in a kind of constant revision, and that living inside that revision requires more energy than it should, even when nothing looks wrong from the outside.

and once that becomes clear, it’s difficult to unsee, because it changes how you understand the interaction entirely as something that removes the possibility of ease, and without that, even the most interesting conversations begin to feel like something you have to work through rather than something you can simply be inside.
